items.q: What is the difference between glacial abrasion and plucking?
items.correct: Abrasion: rock embedded in the glacier base scrapes the bedrock smooth, creating striations. Plucking: meltwater refreezes onto bedrock, then movement rips chunks of rock away.
items.misconception: Abrasion and plucking are different words for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Abrasion is when wind erodes the glacier and plucking is when rain does.
items.why: Both processes act together but produce different evidence: abrasion leaves smooth scratched surfaces; plucking leaves jagged surfaces. Students conflate them — recognising striations vs roches moutonnees is exam-critical.

items.q: How do glaciers form U-shaped valleys?
items.correct: Glaciers erode existing V-shaped river valleys through plucking (freezing onto rock and pulling pieces away) and abrasion (rock embedded in the ice scraping the valley floor and sides).
items.misconception: Glaciers carve U-shaped valleys from flat land by pushing through.
items.random_wrong: U-shaped valleys form when rivers freeze and expand.
items.why: Glaciers usually deepen pre-existing river valleys, not create them from nothing. Students miss that glacial landscapes inherit and modify earlier fluvial topography.

items.q: What drives the movement of tectonic plates?
items.correct: Convection currents in the mantle combined with slab pull at subduction zones and ridge push at constructive boundaries.
items.misconception: The Earth's rotation makes the plates move.
items.random_wrong: Plates move because of ocean tides pulling on the crust.
items.why: Students conflate the Earth spinning with crustal motion. Plate motion is driven by heat-driven convection in the mantle, not rotation — rotation only affects things like the Coriolis effect.

items.q: Which process breaks rock in place without moving it far?
items.why: It's Weathering. “Erosion” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's UK uplands. “Hot deserts” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: By approximately what percent does water expand when it freezes?
items.why: The real figure is 9. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
